conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child. I have one child, Alex, who is 12 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 12 years old.

Now, to assess your eligibility, I need to ask: Are you responsible for Alex? This means do you live with Alex and are you responsible for their day-to-day care (such as providing food, clothing, and ensuring they attend school)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. Alex lives at home with me and I'm responsible for all of their day-to-day care - I provide their meals, clothes, make sure they get to school, all of that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for confirming that.

Next question: Do you normally live in the UK? And is Alex also normally living in the UK with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we both live in the UK. We're based in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you.

Now I need to ask about your income: Do you or your partner (if you have one) have an individual income over £60,000 per year? This is important because there's a High Income Child Benefit Charge that may apply.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I don't have information about my income in the profile I've been given, so I can't really answer that question. Is that something that would stop me from claiming Child Benefit altogether, or is it just about whether I'd have to pay some of it back?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's a very good question! Your income level does **not** stop you from claiming Child Benefit altogether. 

Here's how it works:
- You can claim Child Benefit regardless of your income
- If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the High Income Child Benefit Charge via your tax return
- However, many people in this situation still choose to claim Child Benefit even if they have to pay it back, because it helps protect National Insurance credits (which can count towards your State Pension)

For the purpose of determining your basic **eligibility to claim** Child Benefit, income is not a barrier.

Let me ask another question: Is Alex in full-time non-advanced education or approved training? (At 12 years old, they're likely in secondary school, which would count as full-time education)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex is in secondary school, so yes, full-time education.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

Just a few more questions to make sure I have all the information:

1. Are you a British citizen, or do you have the right to live and work in the UK (such as settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another immigration status that allows you to claim benefits)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I don't have any information about my citizenship or immigration status in my profile, so I can't answer that. Would that affect my eligibility?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Yes, immigration status does affect eligibility for Child Benefit. To be eligible, you generally need to be:
- A British or Irish citizen, OR
- Have settled status (indefinite leave to remain), OR
- Have a specific immigration status that gives you access to public funds

If someone doesn't have the right to claim public funds (for example, if they're in the UK on a visa with a "no recourse to public funds" condition), they would not be eligible for Child Benefit.

However, since you don't have information about your immigration status in your profile, let me ask: For the purposes of this assessment, would you like me to assume you have the right to claim benefits in the UK? Or would you prefer to find out your actual status before I complete the assessment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see. Well, I've lived here all my life and I'm a UK citizen, so that shouldn't be a problem.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for clarifying that!

Just one final question to complete the assessment: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex? (Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the key requirements:
- ✓ You are responsible for Alex who lives with you
- ✓ You provide Alex's day-to-day care
- ✓ Both you and Alex normally live in the UK
- ✓ Alex is 12 years old (under 16)
- ✓ Alex is in full-time education
- ✓ You are a UK citizen
- ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit. As mentioned earlier, if you or a partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through the High Income Child Benefit Charge, but this doesn't prevent you from claiming.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 12 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
